title: Optimal Bounds, Barriers, and Extensions for Non-Hermitian Bivariate Quantum Signal Processing
abstract: Multivariate quantum signal processing (M-QSP) has recently been shown to be applicable for non-Hermitian Hamiltonian simulation, opening several problems regarding the optimization landscape, angle-finding, and constant-factor analysis. We resolve several of these problems here. We find the anti-Hermitian query complexity $d_I = Θ(\betaI T + \log(1/\varepsilon)/\log\log(1/\varepsilon))$ to be tight, established via Chebyshev coefficient bounds, modified Bessel function asymptotics, and Lambert~$W$ inversion. Fast-forwarding to $d_I = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\betaI T})$ is impossible in the bivariate polynomial model, though a linear state-dependent improvement to $d_I = \mathcal{O} β_{\mathrm{eff}} T + \log(1/\varepsilon)/\log\log(1/\varepsilon))$ is achievable. The optimization landscape of M-QSP admits spurious local minima, but a warm-start basin guarantee ensures the two-stage algorithm converges. CRC-exploiting block peeling reduces angle-finding from $\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(d^2)$ classical operations, and optimized error allocation yields a leading constant of approximately~$2$ relative to the information-theoretic lower bound. A constant-ratio condition extends to non-identical signal operators, enabling time-dependent non-Hermitian simulation with query complexity $\mathcal{O}(\int_0^T(\alphaR(s) + \betaI(s))\,ds + \log(1/\varepsilon)/\log\log(1/\varepsilon))$. Block-encoding overhead $e^{-2\betaI T}$ holds across all function classes within the walk-operator oracle model, and dilational methods (Schrödingerization) achieve the walk-operator barrier. A precisely characterized direct-access construction achieves the intrinsic barrier $e^{-2ωT}$ (with $ω< \betaI$ for non-commuting Hamiltonians) on a restricted domain, though extension to the full bitorus remains open.
summary: This paper provides a definitive analysis of Multivariate Quantum Signal Processing for non-Hermitian systems, establishing tight bounds on query complexity and identifying fundamental barriers to simulation. It also introduces efficient algorithms for angle-finding and demonstrates how to handle time-dependent non-Hermitian Hamiltonians.
main_problem: Resolving open problems in Multivariate Quantum Signal Processing (M-QSP) for non-Hermitian Hamiltonian simulation, specifically regarding query complexity, optimization landscapes, and fundamental simulation barriers.
main_result: The paper proves the tightness of anti-Hermitian query complexity, identifies the existence of spurious local minima in the optimization landscape, and establishes a three-tier hierarchy of simulation barriers.
method: The study employs polynomial approximation theory, Chebyshev coefficient bounds, and L-BFGS-B optimization, alongside numerical surveys of random Hamiltonian ensembles (GUE and Wishart).
model_or_system: The model focuses on the simulation of non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonians H_eff = H_R + iH_I, where H_R and H_I are Hermitian, using a bivariate quantum signal processing framework and a walk-operator oracle.
key_observables: Query complexity (d_I, d_R), spectral abscissa (omega), normalization factor (lambda), and the advantage factor (e^{2(beta_I - omega)T}).
important_parameters: Simulation time (T), error tolerance (epsilon), Hermitian norm (alpha_R), anti-Hermitian norm (beta_I), and the spectral-abscissa ratio (omega/beta_I).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a walk-operator oracle model for the primary complexity bounds and considers both commuting and non-commuting regimes of H_R and H_I.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mean spectral-abscissa ratio across different dimensions, the exponential scaling of the advantage factor, and the convergence rates of the optimizer.
paper_structure: The paper begins by addressing complexity and tightness of bounds, moves to analyzing the optimization landscape and angle-finding efficiency, extends the framework to time-dependent Hamiltonians, and concludes with a hierarchy of fundamental simulation barriers.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental limits and algorithmic improvements for simulating dissipative and non-Hermitian dynamics, which are central to open quantum systems, quantum optics, and Lindbladian evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Empirical distribution of the non-commuting direct-access advantage factor e2(βI−ω)T , for the same GUE × Wishart and rank-1 ensembles reported in Table XII (N = 500 samples per cell, seed 42; both HR and HI are operator-norm rescaled to βI = 1). (a) Mean spectral-abscissa ratio ω(Heff)/βI with sample standard deviation (error bars) for system dimensions n ∈{4, 8, 16} and two structural ensembles (dense Wishart vs rank-1 projector). All samples lie in the range ω/βI ∈[0.70, 0.76], below the commuting limit ω = βI (dashed line). (b) Advantage factor at βIT ∈{10, 20}. Solid curves report ensemble median, dotted curves sample maximum; log axis makes the asymptotic e2(βI−ω)T scaling...
